{{Chess Opening Theory/Position|= |Bishop's Opening| |moves=1. e4 e5 2. Bc4 |eco=[[Chess/ECOC|C23]] |parent=[[../|Open game]] |responses=
*[[/2...Nf6|3...Nf6 · Berlin defence]] *[[/2...Bc5|2...Bc5 · Classical defence]]. }} == 2. Bc4 · Bishop's opening == With '''2. Bc4''' White develops their bishop to increase control over d5 and pressure f7, the weakest pawn in Black's position. The Bishop's opening is very transpositional: by not having played Nf3 yet, White retains the option of f4 and may plan to transpose into [[Chess/Vienna Gambit|Vienna]] or [[Chess/King's Gambit|King's gambit]] lines, or the game can be taken into a [[Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...e5/2. Nf3/2...Nf6|Russian]] or [[Chess/Italian Game|Italian]] direction. Compared to [[../2. Nf3|2. Nf3]], White has not threatened Black's e5 pawn, so Black is afforded flexibility in their reply. The main moves are 2...Nf6 or 2...Bc5. [[/2...Nf6|'''2...Nf6''']] and Black attacks White's e pawn. White may defend it or not. They can defend with 3. Nc3, transposing into a [[Chess/Vienna Game|Vienna game]], or 3. d3, happy that their king's bishop is already developed outside of the pawn chain. They can leave it undefended to develop 3. Nf3, transposing into a Russian game, or to counterattack with 3. d4 or 3. f4 (the Ponziani and Greco gambits, respectively). [[/2...Bc5|'''2...Bc5''']] is the classical response, developing Black's bishop. Transpositions to Vienna or Italian games are possible with 3. Nc3 or 3. Nf3. 3. b4 is a gambit resembling the [[Chess/Evans Gambit|Evan's gambit]]. The more original response is 3. c3, supporting an eventual d4 push. In amateur games, 3. Qh5 or 3. Qf3 may follow, expecting that Black will allow Qxf7#. Other second moves for Black include * [[/2...Nc6|'''2...Nc6''']], letting White whether to transpose into the Italian with 3. Nf3 or the Vienna with 3. Nc3, or hold off with 3. d3. * [[/2...f5|'''2...f5!?''']], the Calabrese countergambit, a sharp but not very sound gambit. * [[/2...b5|'''2...b5?!''']], the Anderssen attack, a Romantic but not very sound gambit. * [[/2...c6|'''2...c6''']], the Philidor counterattack, preparing d5, striking in the centre. * [[/2...d5|'''2...d5!?''']], the Khan gambit, to give up a pawn for the bishop pair (if 3. Bxd5 Nf6 4. Nc3 Nxd5 5. exd5) or two pawns for development (if 3. exd5 c6 4. dxc6 Nxc6), or a lead in development (if 3. exd5 Nf6 4. Nc3 Bd6) * 2...d6 to transpose to Philidor's Defence. === History === The Bishop's opening was popular in the 18th Century and a favourite of [[wikipedia:François-André Danican Philidor|Philidor]]. His 1790 book, ''Analysis of the Game of Chess'', largely focuses on this opening. In the 1930s, [[Wikipedia:Weaver Adams|Weaver Adams]] used to claim it was a "forced win" for White, but it's usually considered that Black can equalise with best play.{{Cite book |last=de Firmian |first=Nick |title=Modern Chess Openings |publisher=David McKay Company |year=1999 |isbn=0812930843}} Danish Grandmaster Bent Larsen revived the opening in the 1960s at the [https://www.365chess.com/tournaments/amsterdam_interzonal_1964/26079 1964 Amsterdam Interzonal]. ==Theory table== {{Chess Opening Theory/Table}}.
